content:
https://github.com/w-lfpup/jackrabbit-js
Made Jackrabbit-js because I wanted a test-runner without dependencies for nodejs and the browser without doing olympic levels of effort.
I like using old / smoll hardware. Most options feel heavy. Playwright ships with prepackaged binaries. Puppeteer downloads the latest version of chrome. But we all got browsers locally. And we use containers and CI like GitHub actions. And those containers actually provide webdrivers for nearly every browser.
Sooo why can't I just use the browsers on my computer and in GitHub actions?
And that's what this library does.
As far as syntax goes, passing tests look like this:
function testStuffAndPass() { return; } function testMoreStuffAndPass() { return []; } Failing tests look like this:
function testStuffAndFail() { return "this test failed!"; } function testMoreStuffAndFail() { return ["this test also failed!"]; } Im really not a fan of daisy().chained().builder().syntax().okay("?") lol I think yes !== no looks just fine.
Also if you want to test on a browser? Jackrabbit provides about a dozen spec-compliant webdriver commands.
For instance you can take a screenshot of an element with the following:
import { findElement, takeElementScreenshot } from "@w-lfpup/jackrabbit"; let elementId = await findElement("p"); await takeElementScreenshot(elementId, "./path/relative/to/cwd.png"); The readme explains more about configuring browsers and available webdriver commands.
Jackrabbit actually tests itself in nodejs and every major browser available in GitHub action (which includes a real instance of safari).
The test-runner is decoupled from tests entirely. So you could write your own test-runner without changing your tests. I guess it's an open "test-spec" in that way.
Already using it to test other projects and it's going pretty well. But this is still fairly early. The CLI could change. I'll add more webdriver commands as I run into use-cases. Kinda curious if this fits anyone else's needs too?
Also thanks for reading this!

  submitted by   /u/w-lfpup
[link]   [comments]
